metadata.title: The digital transformation of the public sphere

abstract: In this paper, we study the relationship between social media and traditional media and their impact on democratic systems. We revisit the concepts of the public sphere and agenda-setting and translate them into the current media landscape. Using topic-modelling techniques, we then explore the effects of first level agenda-setting in the Twitter debate on the Brexit referendum. Our results reveal a complex dynamic in the transmission of the media agenda to the public agenda. The traditional media dominated the debate, but not entirely, as alternative media played an important role in the campaign. The "Leave" campaign had more success in the transmission of its interpretative agenda, while the user-generated content polarized the media's message. We conclude a list of recommendations to address the societal challenges posed by the transformation of the media environments.

sections.paper_id: Santos et al. - 2019 - The digital transformation of the public sphere
paragraphs: Recent changes in global politics seem to configure a historical phase dominated by the emergence of populism. The use of social networks in this political cycle, from the UK "Brexit" referendum to the election of Donald Trump and Jair Bolsonaro, has been deeply instrumental and controversial, resulting in a fear that the social networks are deteriorating the quality of democracy (Gerbaudo, 2018;Tucker et al., 2018). The relationship between the social media and the traditional media is complex and problematic but despite the recent changes in media environments, studies show that the traditional media influence not only the relevance of issues in social media, but also at a faster pace (Conway, Kenski, & Wang, 2015;Conway-Silva, Filer, Kenski, & Tsetsi, 2018;Shehata & Stromback, 2013;Vargo, Guo, & Amazeen, 2018). However, there is also evidence that social networks challenge the ability of the traditional media to define the public agenda. Concepts such as "echo chamber", "confirmation bias", or "filter bubbles" have thus become central to the debate on the effects of the social media on the dynamics of opinion formation (Boynton & Richardson, 2015;Meraz, 2011;Neuman, Guggenheim, Mo Jang, & Bae, 2014).

In this paper, we will address the relationship between the social media and the traditional media and their impact on society and democratic systems. To better understand the current transformations in the media space, we start by revisiting Habermas ' (1989) work on the concept of the public sphere and the media transformations that occurred in the past to later move on to its translation into the current media landscape. Subsequently, we use the agenda-setting theory (McCombs & Shaw, 1972) to understand the relationships between the different actors of the public sphere, namely, the social and the traditional media. Based on the theoretical framework defined above, we then undergo an empirical analysis, taking as a case study in the Twitter debate on the Brexit referendum. Finally, we conclude and list possible solutions to the societal challenges addressed in this work.
1.1 | From salon conversations to mass media: the Habermas' public sphere

The concept of the public sphere was initially explored by Habermas in his study "The Structural Change of the Public Sphere" (Habermas, 1989). Habermas mentions the social changes that occurred at the end of the 18th century in Germany, France, and the United Kingdom that gave rise to a bourgeois public sphere. For a brief period, there were social conditions that triggered and facilitated a situation where a large number of middle-class men came together in salons to debate matters of mutual interest, creating a space for rational public debate. Consequently, the emergence of this new public space formed a zone of mediation between the state and the private individual (Roberts & Crossley, 2004).

At the beginning of the 20th century, with the rise of the mass media, such as newspapers and radio, the public sphere came to be dominated by a series of actors, from the state to the most varied representatives of civic and commercial interests, who removed the public from the most direct forms of participation and deliberation (Habermas, 1989). This process, which Habermas identified in the transition from the salons to the mass media, continued until the 1960s, when the media environment changed considerably, putting an end to the dominance of an elite of public and commercial organisations in developed countries. In its place, not only did a range of alternative media emerged at the local and international level, but also media consumption patterns changed, with newspapers and television recording falling audiences in favour of online streaming or on-demand options (Bruns & Highfield, 2016). Most of these changes have been due to the emergence of the Internet as par excellence channel for the dissemination of news. Since the 1990s, as traditional organisations have switched to the online medium, the press and the television lost a substantial share of the news market. More recent emergence of social networks has provided a diversity of spaces for the dissemination and debate of news and public affairs, thus contributing to the mediatization of everyday life, which has increased the complexity of the contemporary media environment. This progressive fusion of national public spheres with global information flows led to the questioning of the concept of a single domestic public sphere. It is true that even in the golden age of the mass media, the public's attention was never uniform, because the members of each audience selected the subjects according to their interests. But a critical re-evaluation of the concept of the public sphere or even its replacement by a model that allows the coexistence of multiple public spheres in competition with each other is not new; it has only been revisited as a result of the emergence of global digital spaces (Bruns & Highfield, 2016). Benkler (2007), for example, develops the idea of a "networked public sphere" that draws inspiration from online communication platforms. The existence of specific terms for each platform, such as blogosphere and Twittersphere, to characterize the activity of users of these social networks, seems to be indicative of the need to extend the concept of the public sphere in this sense. But similarly, we can also have different public spheres defined by the press, the radio, or the television, as they enunciate open flows of communication that enables the relations between socially distant interlocutors. Thus, publics would not be spaces where politics is discussed, but rather interstitial networks of individuals and civic groups. The state, the economy, and the civil society are therefore linked by stable but publicly emerging institutions (Emirbayer & Sheller, 1999).

However, cultural or technocentric definitions may not be very productive. When it comes to critiquing the orthodoxy of the concept of the public sphere, differences in media consumption may indeed give rise to divergent dynamics between newspapers and online audiences, for example. But when we define a public sphere in terms of the technology that supports it, this category will be analytically sterile, because few citizens make exclusive use of a particular technology. The addition of structural categories, such as the networking of online platforms, also does not seem very revealing because, with the progressive mobilization of publics for online media, the distinction between the public networked and "analogue" spheres no longer makes sense. On the other hand, this type of approach leaves out the political economy of the public sphere, but we can expect that, even under favourable conditions of communication, the contribution to the spontaneous formation of the opinion of the masses, economically dependent, will depend on their social independence from the owners of private property (Fuchs, 2013).

sections.paper_id: Santos et al. - 2019 - The digital transformation of the public sphere
sections.header_raw: | Social media, the beginning of the corporate sphere?
sections.header_norm: social media the beginning of the corporate sphere
paragraphs: In the Reuters Digital News Report (Newman, Levy, & Nielsen, 2019), data aggregating the average participation in social networks since 2014 show that 64% of respondents use Facebook, and 36% do so to consult news. At first glance, we could say that the limitations enunciated by Habermas are unfounded since the emergence of social networks democratized access to the public sphere. However, the scandals involving Facebook and YouTube in issues related to the lack of respect for users' privacy and their role in disseminating misinformation and hate speech led to a series of changes in the Facebook algorithm that are already affecting news publishers and consumers. The changes were towards shifting the focus to private messaging platforms, like WhatsApp and Facebook Messenger, which means that news sharing and debate will be less open and transparent in the future. Since 2014, we've seen the rapid growth of these two platforms, along with more recent ones such as Instagram and Snapchat, while Facebook and Twitter have stabilized, although news consumption on Facebook has decreased. On the other hand, the same report underlines that Facebook's position as the dominant company, also holding Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp platforms, allows its products to represent 84% of the sample and 57% of news consumption. Messaging applications such as Viber, Telegram, and WeChat reach 75% overall and 31% in news, with an 8% growth in the last two years. This evidence leads us, once again, to wonder whether the extension of the concept of the public sphere in the sense of encompassing plurality makes sense. Looking at platforms, such as Facebook, as a medium in themselves, we can see that the media environment is evolving towards monopolistic concentration and not towards its democratisation. In the transformation of the public sphere that Habermas characterized in the last century, the mass media occupied the spaces of public debate, forming a zone of mediation between the state and the private individual. Similarly, with the digital transformation of the public sphere, social networks, such as blogs or forums, not only occupied the space of digital public debate, but also the previously established mass media. Nevertheless, the capture of advertising revenues by social media has led to the reformulation of existing media business models towards the addition of paywalls and subscription plans. This is a model that only seems to work in rich countries, such as the US or Nordic countries, and with established publishers, like the NY Times or the Washington Post. According to current trends, most people are not prepared to pay for online news (Newman et al., 2019), and if this course is maintained, we will necessarily have a less diverse and accessible media space, because not only the big publishers will survive, but also most people will not be able to pay for content. In this reconfiguration, the zone of mediation between the state and the public will tend to disappear. On the one hand, there are no competing state platforms; on the other hand, the consumption of news from the state media is assimilated by corporations, which are governed by market forces. Additionally, the clear difficulty that the state has in regulating the activity of companies like Facebook, with the most visible result of which, is its monopolistic market position (BIS Annual Economic Report, 2019). These trends lead us to conclude that the current set of structural transformations in the public sphere is moving towards its replacement by a corporate sphere.

sections.header_raw: | Social media and traditional media: a complex relationship
sections.header_norm: social media and traditional media a complex relationship
paragraphs: In 1962, shortly after the first publication of Habermas' work about the public sphere, Maxwell McCombs, and Donald Shaw carried out their seminal study of Chapel Hill, which measured, during the US presidential elections, the relationship between patterns of news coverage of public affairs and their perception amongst voters (McCombs & Shaw, 1972). The hypothesis stated that the prominence of issues published by the media influences the salience of these issues on the public agenda. That is, the media agenda defines the public agenda, being the term agenda relative to the pattern of news coverage of the issues (Boynton & Richardson, 2015). Since then, the agenda-setting theory is one of the main perspectives in the study of the effects on mass communication and explains the role of the media in the processes of public opinion formation (Takeshita, 2006). The agendasetting theory arises as a function of the mediation zone between the public and the state defined by the public sphere. It assumes that the media is the channel by which voters have access to the candidates. In this sense, the media have a prominent role in the formation of political reality, given that they are the curators of the information available to voters. Consequently, the effects of the agenda-setting are determined by the correlation between the media and the public agendas that measure the media's success in transferring campaign issues to the public agenda.

The relationship between the social networks and the traditional media is complex and problematic, but despite the recent transformations in media environments (McCombs, 2005), the traditional media continue not only to influence the highlighting of issues in social networks, but also at a faster pace (Conway et al., 2015;Conway-Silva et al., 2018;Shehata & Stromback, 2013;Vargo et al., 2018). However, there is also evidence that the social media challenges the capacity of the traditional media to define the interpretive agenda (Boynton & Richardson, 2015;Meraz, 2011;Neuman et al., 2014). The amount of information produced on the social media greatly exceeds our capacity to consume it, thus generating a competition for attention that has an economic value. Optimizing the experience of users on social media plays, therefore, a key role in their business model; to this end, these companies adopt algorithmic strategies aimed at filtering the information to which we are exposed. One of the fears is that these methods, which are essentially black boxes, may affect opinion formation and its dynamics. These concerns are widespread, and concepts such as "echo chamber", "confirmation bias", or "filter bubbles" have become central to the debate on the effects of algorithmic filtering on the opinion-forming dynamics. The literature on the coverage of elections on social networks reveals partisanship, political polarization, and misinformation, which seems to corroborate these fears (Gorodnichenko, Pham, & Talavera, 2018;Perra & Rocha, 2018).

Nevertheless, data from the 2016 US presidential study (Faris et al., 2017) support the polarization of the US political spectrum as a consequence of asymmetric media journalistic practices. While conservative media have focussed on the Trump campaign and highly sectarian sources such as Breitbart, the liberal side has relied on long-established media organisations grounded in the traditions and practices of objective journalism. The structure of the ecosystem of right-wing media sites, with its relative insularity and densely interconnected network, may have contributed to its efficiency in attracting the attention of users to sites dedicated to conspiracy theories, such as Gateway Pundit or InfoWars, and sensationalist sites, such as Ending the Fed. This network structure is more conducive to echo-chamber effects, information cascades, or filter bubbles, but being structural effects, the same could also apply to media ecosystems on the left. For these authors, the problem is essentially political, because as long as extreme and sensationalist messages win votes and yield advertising profits to the networks that propagate them, such dynamics will never fade away. In this sense, the issues related to the production, dissemination, consumption, and processing of political information are political acts. In this case, if the challenges come from a predominant wing of the American political system, the solution is far from obvious, and any intervention will necessarily have to confront the political origins of the problem (Faris et al., 2017).

Only a dialectical understanding of the relationship between technology and society can provide a free of any determinism. This means that societal conditions, interests, and conflicts influence the emergence of a given technology, but its effects are not predetermined. Modern technologies are a complex whole made up of parts that interact with each other, and they are, to some extent, unpredictable. At a time when profound technological changes are taking place in society, it is not difficult to give in to some kind of technodeterminist explanation, but technology is only a means and a consequence of society (Fuchs, 2013).

In 1962, shortly after the first publication of Habermas' work about the public sphere, Maxwell McCombs, and Donald Shaw carried out their seminal study of Chapel Hill, which measured, during the US presidential elections, the relationship between patterns of news coverage of public affairs and their perception amongst voters (McCombs & Shaw, 1972). The hypothesis stated that the prominence of issues published by the media influences the salience of these issues on the public agenda. That is, the media agenda defines the public agenda, being the term agenda relative to the pattern of news coverage of the issues (Boynton & Richardson, 2015). Since then, the agenda-setting theory is one of the main perspectives in the study of the effects on mass communication and explains the role of the media in the processes of public opinion formation (Takeshita, 2006). The agendasetting theory arises as a function of the mediation zone between the public and the state defined by the public sphere. It assumes that the media is the channel by which voters have access to the candidates. In this sense, the media have a prominent role in the formation of political reality, given that they are the curators of the information available to voters. Consequently, the effects of the agenda-setting are determined by the correlation between the media and the public agendas that measure the media's success in transferring campaign issues to the public agenda.

sections.paper_id: Santos et al. - 2019 - The digital transformation of the public sphere
sections.header_raw: | The Brexit referendum debate on Twitter
sections.header_norm: the brexit referendum debate on twitter
paragraphs: As a case study, we used the Twitter discussion on the referendum held on June 23, 2016 in the United Kingdom (UK). Better known as the "Brexit" referendum, it divided the country into two camps, the "Remain" and the "Leave", which included, respectively, those for and against the continuation of the UK in the EU. At the end of the campaign, the field in favour of leaving the EU won with 52% of the votes. The campaign was the most divisive, hostile, and negative that ever took place in the UK, characterized by hyper-partisan coverage by the national media, which not only publicly expressed their position, but also selected and framed news, editorials, and highlights according to it. The only exceptions were the public information services such as the BBC, which are required by law to be neutral (Moore & Ramsay, 2017).

Our sample comprised of 1,163,311 tweets with the keyword "brexit" and corresponds to the time interval of the year preceding the referendum (June 2015-July 2016). From this set, we selected the tweets that contained links to other sites, which constitute 360,106 links from 57,390 domains, of which we chose only the 20 top-level domains in terms of the number of shared links. We did this, on the one hand for the sake of feasibility; on the other hand, because they are the most present domains, representing close to 20% of all tweets, from which were generated approximately 25% of the links (Table 1). This concentration is quite impressive, especially if we consider that these 20 domains constitute 0.03% of the domains in the sample. The interpretation of links to other sites on social networks can have many meanings: it can signal endorsement or disagreement and can combine elements of trust, novelty, or credit, amongst others; so that we can also collect information regarding sharing within Twitter (retweets) and favourites. Afterward, we scraped the web pages shared in the tweets, extracting their titles and body text. From the data collected, for each of the 20 domains, we created three subsamples with the text of the tweets, the titles, and the body text of the shared pages. Then, using an unsupervised automatic learning method, we revealed the latent topics in each of the subsamples. We chose latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA), a probabilistic generative model that presents the topics as multinomial word distributions, where each document is described as a mixture of topics (Blei et al., 2003). Previous studies have revealed that the documents with few characters, such as tweets, may not contain enough information to build robust automatic learning models. In this sense, the same studies recommend training topic models in aggregated documents to improve performance (Steinskog, Therkelsen, & Gambäck, 2017), a recommendation that we took into account.

To find the agenda-setting effects, we performed three sets of analyses, assessing the similarity between the sets by calculating the Euclidean distance between the topic models, where the greater distance represents less similarity. First, we calculated the similarity between the title and the body text of the articles of the web pages with the text of the tweets. Given that the structural difference between the text of the tweets (140 characters), and the text body of the articles could lead to inconclusive results, we also compared the titles of the articles that normally have the same structure of the tweets, with the text body. In this way, we were able to ensure that the results derived from their semantic meaning and not from their structural differences. We can thus infer that in the media where the distribution of article topics is similar to that of tweets, there is a tacit acceptance by users of the media message.

Second, we determined the similarity between the tweets, the titles, and the text of each medium with the total sample of tweets (with and without shared connections). This approach will later allow us to identify to what extent the message of a given media is representative of the general opinion of our sample.
Finally, to understand if there is any type of pattern or relationship between the results obtained in the previous experience, we produced dendograms based on a hierarchical cluster analysis method.

Concerning the results, thirteen traditional media are present in our sample, a clear sign that these still dominate the media space, a result that is in line with previous studies (Gorrell et al., 2018). However, the presence of Breitbart, a site of the American extreme right that was instrumental in the election of Donald Trump (Faris 1) Retweets Favourites Unique links % (2) Articles (3) % (3) The Guardian Traditional Remain 41,100 3,5 82,017 56,215 11,988 3,3 10,898 90,9 Financial Times (FT) Traditional 16,980 1,5 27,401 17,196 9758 2,7 4487 46 The Independent Traditional 9463 0,8 27,539 17,339 2494 0,7 2386 95,7 The Economist Traditional 3821 0,3 25,018 19,415 845 0,2 687 81,3 Daily Mirror Traditional 3295 0,3 16,482 8927 1377 0,4 867 63 Subtotal 74,659 6,4 178,457 119,092 26,462 7,3 19,325 The Telegraph Traditional Leave 26,587 2,3 73,258 48,820 8151 2,3 6913 84,8 Express Traditional 19,412 1,7 100,051 60,629 8538 2,4 8053 94,3 The Daily Mail Traditional 15,227 1,3 45,525 28,755 7816 2,2 7552 96,6 Breitbart Partisan 6561 0,6 35,286 23,689 2499 0,7 1802 72,1 Subtotal 67,787 5,9 254,120 161,893 27,004 7,6 24,320 BBC Traditional Neutral 22,175 1,9 37,602 27,165 10,139 2,8 3622 35,7 Sky Traditional 5061 0,4 11,589 8761 1193 0,3 834 69,9 Sub-total 27,236 2,3 49,191 35,926 11,332 3,1 4456 Bloomberg Emerging -10,951 0,9 30,226 16,729 1884 0,5 1006 53,4 Reuters News agency 10,406 0,9 15,738 10,480 9836 2,7 9284 94,4 NY Times Traditional 4146 0,4 6590 6971 982 0,3 918 93,5 LinkedIn Social media 4126 0,4 2278 1910 712 0,2 590 82,9 Yahoo Emerging 3921 0,3 2326 1725 3110 0,9 2604 83,7 Russia Today (RT) News agency 3642 0,3 10,937 7211 1908 0,5 1431 75 Politico Emerging 3299 0,3 9137 5593 714 0,2 656 91,9 CNBC Traditional 3099 0,3 4436 2943 880 0,2 769 87,4 Wall Street Journal (WSJ) Traditional 2986 0,3 9560 6979 1811 0,5 1543 85,2 Subtotal 46,576 4,1 91,228 60,541 21,837 6 18,801 Total 216,258 18,6 86,635 24,1 66,902 et al. , 2017), amongst the 10 most shared, shows that alternative media are in a position to defy the traditional media. Regarding the positioning, five traditional media showed their support for the "Remain" field, while three chose the "Leave" field (Moore & Ramsay, 2017). Grouping the media according to this preference, the results reveal that the "Remain" field dominates in terms of the number of tweets, but captures less attention from users, having fewer retweets and favourites. This is especially visible when comparing The Guardian to The Express. The latter has almost half the number of tweets but captures more retweets and favourites.

Figure 1 summarizes the first set of experiences, where we calculated the similarity between the title and the text of the articles and the text of the tweets for each medium. The results show that the titles of the articles are more similar to the tweets. As we have already seen, this result may derive from the structural similarities between the titles and the 140-character anatomy of the tweet, or even from the fact that some extensions to web browsers and social network management programs use the page title as a default for sharing. The correlation of the distance between the article text and tweets (dashed line), and the distance between article titles and text is Pearson: 0.99|r2: 98.0

sections.preview_head: As a case study, we used the Twitter discussion on the referendum held on June 23, 2016 in the United Kingdom (UK). Better known as the "Brexit" referendum, it divided the country into two camps, the "Remain" and the "Leave", which included, respectively, those for and against the continuation of the UK in the EU. At the end of the campaign, the field in favour of leaving the EU won with 52% of the votes.

paragraphs: The Euclidean distance between media's tweets (solid line), article titles (dashed line) and text (punctuated line), and all tweets in the dataset practically the same, with a Pearson correlation of 0.99. For this reason, it seems safe to conclude that the results are representative of the semantic similarities between corpora. Based on this premise, we can state that, in general, the titles of the articles reflect the message conveyed in the article, and that the users, by changing the initial message, distanced themselves from it. In this way, less similar corpora reveal a lower level of acceptance of the message of a given medium by the users. This does not necessarily mean that users disagreed with the message, but that they only felt the need to add information to it, either to reinforce it or to subvert it. Media such as The Guardian, BBC, The Telegraph, or The Daily Mail thus show less similarity to users' tweets. On the other hand, users who have shared links to CNBC, WSJ, LinkedIn, The NY Times, Bloomberg, The Economist, or RT tend to accept the messages in the titles of these media. It seems to us that, in a campaign where the economy was the dominant issue (Moore & Ramsay, 2017), the media specialized in this issue (except the Financial Times) tend to be less contested than the generalist media. This trend may be explained by the fact that in the UK there is a general perception that the media are biassed (YouGov, 2018).

In the second experiment (Figure 2), concerning the similarities between the media and the entire corpus of tweets, we observe that the similarities between the titles and the tweets follow the trend described above. But surprisingly, the text of the articles is generally more representative of users' opinions. From the previous experience, one would expect the opposite, the titles of the articles should be more similar to tweets. One hypothesis is that users use the title in the text when they share the article on Twitter, but by discussing the themes, they end up reproducing what they read in the text body of the articles. This hypothesis was not tested in this study, but in any case, it seems to us that we are facing a complex construction of opinion on Twitter. The media closest to the general opinion are The Guardian, The Telegraph, The Daily Mail, BBC, and The Independent and are precisely the ones whose message has been most adulterated by users. In the opposite direction, the less FIGURE 4 Dendogram of the Euclidean distances between the These results are in line with the previous studies (Ceron, Curini, & Iacus, 2016;Meraz, 2011), which reveal that the traditional media, by highlighting the issues that Twitter users debate, can dictate the agenda of issues that the public should address (first level agenda-setting). However, this does not necessarily mean that they can define how the public should think about this agenda (second level agenda-setting). In this case, and knowing the outcome of the referendum, this is especially applicable to the media that supported the Remain side. These media were effective in conveying their media agenda to the public. This is revealed not only by the high number of tweets with links to these media but also by the domain observed in the distribution of topics. However, its interpretative agenda was not accepted accordingly, with the Leave campaign being more effective in this respect, and getting more attention from users (more retweets and favourites). From the dendograms produced for this experiment (Figures 3, 4, 5), we can see a remarkable compression of information. While in Figure 3 the media have a gradual distribution along the axis that represents similarity, in Figure 5, not only does the length of the axis increase (120 k), but also most of the media occupy the first third of the same axis. Analysing Figure 4, where we compare the similarity between the text of the media articles, the structural difference of the text is observed by the increase of the axis size (200 k), but even here there is a more or less equitable distribution of distances between media. In this sense, we can again state that the differences derived from the semantic meaning of the text and not from its structure. This compression of the distance between media, while creating a gap that isolates The Guardian, seems to indicate a Twitter speech polarization, which is in line with the echo chamber effects previously described (Gorodnichenko et al., 2018;Gorrell et al., 2018). One possible explanation for these results may be that the "Leave" campaign was successful in transmitting its interpretative agenda, framing the messages of some media in its terms while distancing itself from other media, which explains the proximity of Breitbart to The Independent and the distance from The Guardian.

sections.paper_id: Santos et al. - 2019 - The digital transformation of the public sphere
paragraphs: In our study, we explored the effects of the first level agenda-setting in the Twitter debate on the Brexit referendum. The results indicate that the traditional media dominated the debate, but not entirely, as alternative partisan media, such as Breitbart, played an important role in the campaign. Concerning the dynamics of interaction with the content published by the shared media, the comparison between tweets and content from other media suggests that the news headlines have more similarities with the users' opinion than the text of the articles. However, when we compare the information from other media shared on Twitter with the entire debate, the text of the articles is closer to the general opinion. These results seem to indicate a complex dynamic in the transmission of the media agenda to the public agenda which, in our opinion, would deserve more attention in future works. Finally, we observed that the user-generated content contributed to the polarization of the media message, and that the "Leave" campaign had more success in the transmission of its interpretative agenda.

Our study has some limitations. First of all, as this is a small sample of the political debate on the Brexit Twitter referendum, we cannot generalize the results or assume that they are fully representative of the dynamics of public opinion formation on Twitter. But even if we were facing a sample that encompassed the entire debate on this platform, some reservations would have to be made, because in the Reuters Digital News Report of 2019 only 21% of respondents report the use of Twitter, and only 10% of these do so to consult news (Newman et al., 2019). However, other studies reveal that this platform is very representative of public opinion, with data showing that approximately 85% of the trending topics correspond to real news titles (Kwak, Lee, Park, & Moon, 2010), which gives us some confidence in the transposition of our results to reality. Second, the unsupervised automatic learning techniques used can be improved or reviewed. We may add embedding (Li et al., 2017) or apply a nonnegative matrix factorisation algorithm (Shi, Kang, Choo, & Reddy, 2018) instead of LDA. We could also improve confidence in the results by applying the methods used to other policy events and data sets to verify the reproducibility of the results presented.

Today, "big tech" companies are becoming central to democratic processes, providing public spaces for discussion and political contestation, but these companies are governed by market principles. At the outset, they are neutral, being only vehicles of political content. However, they are not only involved in making these contents profitable, but also in editorial decisions aimed at boosting their profits, whether they admit it publicly or not. Take the example of The Guardian's report on Facebook documents made public (Kreiss & Mcgregor, 2018). Howard et al. admit that it is not possible to prevent malicious agents from using computer advertising (Howard, Kelly, & François, 2018). But in a democracy, there may be rules that discourage their use, so these authors suggest the development of robust rules to regulate the use of social media, and APIs that allow researchers to analyse important trends in public life. These would be important developments, but as we have seen, the societal impact of technology companies on the economy and political communication requires, in our opinion, the introduction of transnational regulation to mitigate its effects on democratic systems. This regulatory requirement is becoming evident and is mirrored in the Bank for International Settlements (BIS) report, which reflects concerns over the stability of the global financial system and the protection of consumer data (BIS Annual Economic Report, 2019). In this sense, we listed below a series of measures that could contribute to the solution of some of the current problems

• Introduction of transnational regulation to prevent the current market monopoly of big tech companies such as Google and Facebook; • Development of standards for the development of digital platforms, so that they can be later audited by public entities;

• Availability of open and consistent APIs, with accurate information on the data provided, including the exact relative sample size and relevant statistics, such as the number of views of published content; • Limitations to publication APIs, increasing the time lag between limits and reducing the number of publications per limit. Using the Twitter as an example, it is now possible to automatically create 300 tweets or retweets over 3 hour windows ("Rate Limits: Chart," 2019). Although access to publishing APIs may be of economic interest to companies, the current limits allow the injection of a volume of information that can affect and distort the dynamics of opinion formation; • Creation of decentralized alternative public services.

The pervasiveness and importance that social networks are such that they have become indispensable in an information society, but are also likely to be established as natural monopolies. From this point of view, it would make sense to create public services that ensure healthy competition with existing private provision. This is an idea that will certainly face strong resistance from certain sectors of society, and we could even build a reasonable counter-argument based on the dangers inherent in placing sensitive information of citizens under state control. However, the worst-case scenarios already exist with multinational companies acting with total impunity and in collusion with governments (Kreiss & Mcgregor, 2018). Ideally, it would be desirable for decentralized citizen initiatives such as Mastodon ("Mastodon," 2019), had a higher level of adoption. However, given the disproportionate competition they face from large multinationals, a viable alternative would be state support for the creation, regulation, or at least, sustainability of such alternatives, provided that the necessary measures are taken to ensure the security of citizens' data.

There is a vast body of literature dedicated to the study of social media; however, we think that much of the work in this area is only just beginning. Not only the technological developments and pervasiveness of social media are recent, but the relative stability in the occidental democracies didn't allow us to glimpse the real impact of social media on the democratic systems (Groshek & Koc-Michalska, 2017). Nevertheless, it should be remembered that the study of the effects of technology on society cannot be done without the political economy of events; otherwise, we will fall into the obscurantism of technological determinism that covers up the real dynamics of society (Fuchs, 2013). Technology is embedded in the society, and it is the people who transform it according to their will.

We may add embedding (Li et al., 2017) or apply a nonnegative matrix factorisation algorithm (Shi, Kang, Choo, & Reddy, 2018) instead of LDA. We could also improve confidence in the results by applying the methods used to other policy events and data sets to verify the reproducibility of the results presented.

Today, "big tech" companies are becoming central to democratic processes, providing public spaces for discussion and political contestation, but these companies are governed by market principles. At the outset, they are neutral, being only vehicles of political content. However, they are not only involved in making these contents profitable, but also in editorial decisions aimed at boosting their profits, whether they admit it publicly or not. Take the example of The Guardian's report on Facebook documents made public (Kreiss & Mcgregor, 2018). Howard et al. admit that it is not possible to prevent malicious agents from using computer advertising (Howard, Kelly, & François, 2018). But in a democracy, there may be rules that discourage their use, so these authors suggest the development of robust rules to regulate the use of social media, and APIs that allow researchers to analyse important trends in public life. These would be important developments, but as we have seen, the societal impact of technology companies on the economy and political communication requires, in our opinion, the introduction of transnational regulation to mitigate its effects on democratic systems. This regulatory requirement is becoming evident and is mirrored in the Bank for International Settlements (BIS) report, which reflects concerns over the stability of the global financial system and the protection of consumer data (BIS Annual Economic Report, 2019). In this sense, we listed below a series of measures that could contribute to the solution of some of the current problems

• Introduction of transnational regulation to prevent the current market monopoly of big tech companies such as Google and Facebook; • Development of standards for the development of digital platforms, so that they can be later audited by public entities;

• Availability of open and consistent APIs, with accurate information on the data provided, including the exact relative sample size and relevant statistics, such as the number of views of published content; • Limitations to publication APIs, increasing the time lag between limits and reducing the number of publications per limit. Using the Twitter as an example, it is now possible to automatically create 300 tweets or retweets over 3 hour windows ("Rate Limits: Chart," 2019). Although access to publishing APIs may be of economic interest to companies, the current limits allow the injection of a volume of information that can affect and distort the dynamics of opinion formation; • Creation of decentralized alternative public services.

The pervasiveness and importance that social networks are such that they have become indispensable in an information society, but are also likely to be established as natural monopolies. From this point of view, it would make sense to create public services that ensure healthy competition with existing private provision. This is an idea that will certainly face strong resistance from certain sectors of society, and we could even build a reasonable counter-argument based on the dangers inherent in placing sensitive information of citizens under state control. However, the worst-case scenarios already exist with multinational companies acting with total impunity and in collusion with governments (Kreiss & Mcgregor, 2018). Ideally, it would be desirable for decentralized citizen initiatives such as Mastodon ("Mastodon," 2019), had a higher level of adoption. However, given the disproportionate competition they face from large multinationals, a viable alternative would be state support for the creation, regulation, or at least, sustainability of such alternatives, provided that the necessary measures are taken to ensure the security of citizens' data.

There is a vast body of literature dedicated to the study of social media; however, we think that much of the work in this area is only just beginning. Not only the technological developments and pervasiveness of social media are recent, but the relative stability in the occidental democracies didn't allow us to glimpse the real impact of social media on the democratic systems (Groshek & Koc-Michalska, 2017). Nevertheless, it should be remembered that the study of the effects of technology on society cannot be done without the political economy of events; otherwise, we will fall into the obscurantism of technological determinism that covers up the real dynamics of society (Fuchs, 2013). Technology is embedded in the society, and it is the people who transform it according to their will.
sections.preview_head: In our study, we explored the effects of the first level agenda-setting in the Twitter debate on the Brexit referendum. The results indicate that the traditional media dominated the debate, but not entirely, as alternative partisan media, such as Breitbart, played an important role in the campaign. Concerning the dynamics of interaction with the content published by the shared media, the comparison between tweets and content from other media suggests that the news headlines have more similarities with the users' opinion than the text of the articles.
